messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Detail the dissemination plan, exploitation roadmap, IP strategy, and open access commitments.
Write the Dissemination, exploitation and communication section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.2 M € - - In short, we have laid the foundation for achieving the expected impact of our project: an innovative research agenda, a resourceful action plan for the contextualisation and exploitation of the expected results, and an excellent conglomerate of actors in our consortium who are in pursuit of a shared common goal. 

2.2 Measures to maximise impacts Our vision is to lead the transition towards a sustainable post-petroleum society in the plastic sector while decoupling economic growth from resource depletion and environmental impact by serving industry with the provision of cost-effective high performance sustainable multifunctional bioplastic packaging solutions with tailored materials and organic recyclability and biogas potential at EoL. In order to assist Europe in the transition towards a bio-economy, we will establish new sustainable cross-sectorial value chains providing competitive bio-based alternatives in lightweight packaging, such as nets and trays and textile applications. In line with a number of EU and national regulations, we will address the use of non-biodegradable shopping bags and lightweight multipurpose bags as well as those banning potentially polluting single-use items, e.g. disposable tableware (e.g. in France), but also other enforcement measures for the broader EU SET plan 137, combined with increasing incentives for significant bio-sourcing (e.g. in Germany). There is a clear need for new materials with high bio-based content and for meeting the demand for plastics with innovative EoL options. In order to realise this vision, we have carefully planned post-project exploitation pathways already during our BIOnTOP proposal preparation. We have devised our initial strategy for the dissemination and the exploitation of our project results, with a view to increasing our projects’ innovation & business outcome as well as social & environmental impacts. 

Outline of the dissemination plan Our dissemination plan for BIOnTOP comprises a set of actions to spread the non-confidential results generated during our project to ensure that as many relevant audiences as possible have access to them. The aim underpinning our dissemination strategy is to heighten awareness about the tangible and intangible outputs of BIOnTOP, to contribute to knowledge building and advancement of innovation in Europe as well as to avoid duplication of research work. In order to safeguard the commercial opportunity, our dissemination activities will be subject to a confidentiality policy. By default, and building on our consortium experience in collaborative projects, any result of our project is to be treated as confidential and can be disseminated only after agreement of the project partners following the relevant clauses of our consortium agreement (signed during the grant agreement preparation phase in keeping with initial agreements on ownership reflected herein and respecting the general terms of the grant agreement). This approach allows us to retain complete control and make sure that no “exploitable” information is disclosed before being protected. The protection of the IPR is of invaluable importance for us, the participating partners. Therefore, the non-confidential results and information on our project to be publicly disseminated will be carefully identified. The RTD participants in our consortium will publish results in peer-reviewed open access journals, which will be an indicator of overall project success (in terms of progress vs. the scientific state of the art) and an important element of dissemination towards industry, standardisation bodies, and policy makers. Our industrial partners and market actors will focus on showcasing and disseminating results at conferences and trade fairs and through other industrial channels such as technical/sectorial magazines, industry network meetings, and the like (to demonstrate the industrial novelty and commercial potential of our project outcomes). The IPR Manager (Dr. Marco de la Feld from ENCO) will work closely with us, the project innovators, to manage the identification of non-confidential disclosable information that can be published/disseminated. He will lead us, the project partners, in strategically planning and effectively implementing the dissemination during a dedicated task in our Work Plan, Task 7.5 Exploitation and Dissemination Planning. All the measures (outlined in table 10) will be tailored taking into account our project needs, the target groups’ background and the expertise of our project partners. Our project dissemination activities will intersect dissemination strategies of the BBI-JU, via full members BioMi and PLANET (in process) and associated BIC members AIMPLAS, INSTM, CTB and EUBP, as well as other EU PPP as SPIRE through IRIS and ENCO, which will contribute to the distribution of our project outputs across the relevant European and international communities and amplify our project dissemination effort. All our dissemination activities will be developed with an eye towards the exploitation of our project results by other parties, in order to lay down the basis for subsequent innovation actions as well as other private initiatives which can accelerate the route towards the take-up of new technologies within the bio-based plastic sector, also beyond our selected applications. Beyond the above discussed use of traditional dissemination channels (presentations & posters at conferences, publications, industry magazines, newsletters and online platforms), we will also tap into more vanguard channels, such as social media platforms (ResearchGate, Academia.edu, SlideShare, Twitter, YouTube, Vision2020 open innovation platform), etc. Also, our project website will serve as a lasting virtual hub for disseminating downloadable public deliverables and outputs of BIOnTOP, as it will house also the ‘BIOnTOP Green Channel’ for open access to the peer-reviewed publications of our project. In particular, the network of EUBP across the European bioplastics value chain should be highly instrumental in identifying and approaching prospective business partners and potential licensees. With around 70 member companies from across the bioplastics value chain that are active in the European market, EUBP works closely together with all major stakeholders in this industry. The association also has access to several unparalleled channels of communication within the bioplastics industry, ranging from the largest European bioplastics conference, to the bioplastics-focused newsletter with the widest circulation, to one of the most widely-followed (4000+ followers) Twitter accounts focusing on bioplastics topics. To amplify our impacts, we will also capitalise on ISWA’s (in our AB) communication channels to reach the waste management sector.

2.2.2 IPR management 
Our IPR management of the FreeMe project will be based on three pillars: Documentation of agreements, direct actions for the management of the IP, and necessary interactive activities. The two legal documents defining the main aspects of our IPR management are the Grant Agreement (GA) signed between the EC and us and the Consortium Agreement (CA) signed among us. In the latter, all aspects related to the background knowledge of us that are necessary for the implementation of our project as well as for the post-project exploitation of the results will be defined in detail. In general, we have already agreed that access to the background knowledge, IPRs, and research data will be royalty-free during our project implementation, while the post-project exploitation will be done under fair conditions.  
The general IP management actions will be undertaken by Incotec. The ownership of the foreground knowledge will be depicted in the final “Result Ownership List” (ROL) and will be decided between us consensually based on which partners have been involved in the generation of these results. The communication between the IPR manager and us through appropriate meetings and questionnaires will define efficiently the foreground knowledge. In general, the owner of the IP belongs to the partner that develops it. Joint ownership will be accepted in the case that the results have been jointly generated; however, in this case, the joint owners will agree on the allocation and terms of exploitation of this joint ownership in communication with Incotec and the Coordinator. We will be fully responsible for the protection of our foreground ownership in respect to our own legitimate interests, as well as the legitimate interests of the other partners. We have already agreed that results with a promising commercial or industrial exploitation capacity will be protected. All the IPR updates as well as the Key Results and their IP routes presented in the business case will be included in the PEDR deliverables in terms of Task 8.3.  
Regarding the interactive activities with our consortium, an internal IPR seminar will be organized for the presentation of the different IP protection types and exploitation routes for each Key Result in the first year (M6) as well as a final IPR seminar on M46 for the presentation of the ROL. Bottle closures: The main industry segments for bottle closures are food and beverages packaging and pharmaceuticals. The main material used for bottle closures and caps is plastic, over 54% in 2020, followed by metal, increasing market position due to being more sustainable and durable than plastic, and other materials, such as wood or glass. •Multifunctional rubber panels: The global natural rubber market demand reached a volume of 15.2 million metric tons in 2020. The demand is expected to grow at a CAGR of 4.8% in the forecast period of 2021-2026. The demand for natural rubber is expected to reach a volume of 20.1 million metric tons by 2026. The Rubber sheets industry is characterized for having intense competition and producers. Key money-spinning factors are driving the restraints for the growing market which can be overpassed with strategic planning, as stated in the report.  
•Wood composite bearings: The composite bearings market is expected to grow at a CAGR of over 6% during 2020-2025. The growth in demand for composites bearings from the automotive industry is driving the market. Nevertheless, high raw material costs for manufacturing composite bearings and unfavorable conditions arising due to the COVID-19 outbreak are hindering the growth of the market. The sector that will govern the global market for composites bearing is automotive, with Asia-Pacific being the largest region and the fastest-growing market.  

Business model  
The different business models for our GREEN-LOOP results will target construction, automotive, and food and beverage, the main market segments globally for the upscale of our developed products and technologies, as shown in the different reports. Other promising segments will be further explored as agriculture, healthcare, and cosmetics within our project’s implementation, finding potential applications beyond our project’s life. Specific value propositions will be defined based on delivering the value of the innovative sustainable products at an appropriate and competitive cost. 2.2 Measures to maximise impact Measures to maximise impact will be delivered via WP7 Communication, Dissemination and Exploitation, led by accelCH and closely supported by NEBIH, NP and ROMER. Support and feedback on the planned measures and their long-term impact for commercial and non-commercial exploitation will be received from a dedicated Innovation Manager. The primary goal of our multi-disciplinary and cross-sectorial consortium in WP7 is to disseminate the research results generated in our scientific work packages (WP1-WP6), to raise awareness and provide information on our novel photon-based technology, through communication and outreach activities, and to pave the way for exploitation of the PHOTONFOOD innovations and IP for medium and long-term non-commercial and commercial purposes such as detection and analysis of (micro)biological and chemical contamination along the food production chain from ‘farm to fork’. An overview of our dissemination, exploitation, and communication activities is detailed in the following sections (see 2.2.1 and 2.2.2). 

2.2.1 Dissemination and exploitation of results To maximise impact, we have defined a project-specific dissemination, exploitation and communication strategy that builds on the following three main points. (1) Stakeholder involvement and target-group orientation: To ensure that the interests and requirements of external audiences are fully taken into consideration during the delivery of our project. Along the whole food value chain ’from farm to fork’, we will actively involve representatives of our key target audiences (farmers and novel food producers, food processors, food retailers and wholesalers, analytical labs, EU entry points, technology providers, photonic companies), from the beginning of the project to ensure technology acceptance (WP1), validation in a realistic setting (WP6) and outreach (WP7). The proposed target-group orientation allows us to define and implement measures that are geared to the specific needs of the different audiences (Fig. 12). Additionally, PHOTONFOOD will be in close contact with relevant food safety authorities and will work towards strengthening the community, uniting many important players in the food supply chain. We will aim to collect feedback from interested and influential stakeholders, as well as related projects, and disseminate our results and achievements to them, in order to maximise impact. (2) Cross-channel and multi-media approach: To guarantee the widest possible outreach, we will use different channels to inform about our project and to disseminate research results and innovation and technology developments. These range from direct or face-to-face contact with scientists at conferences and webinars and industrial workshops organized in WP1 to indirect information distributed to all stakeholders. Additionally, we will take advantage of print media, digital tools and platforms for our dissemination, training, and communication activities. Some of our digital outreach highlights include video clips, open webinars, education and marketing videos. (3) Open access and open data policy: To enable the future uptake of PHOTONFOOD’s research results and to facilitate future sustainability of the materials and technologies, we are fully committed to open access publishing, following the Guidelines on Open Access to Scientific Publications and Research Data in Horizon 2020 that are in line with current trends in IP management and publication strategies (see section 2.2.1.3). This will be achieved by (i) applying existing standards for the development and results publication, (ii) involving stakeholders as of the beginning of the action and (iii) publishing project results in peer-reviewed journals while ensuring open access. 

Plan for dissemination and exploitation of results The WP7 leader accelCH will coordinate and monitor the related tasks and closely cooperate with all project partners and external collaborators involved. We will be responsible for monitoring and coordination of the implementation of the defined outreach strategy, through the dissemination activities, exploitation plans, and communication actions at the consortium level with all work package leaders offering support related to dissemination, communication and exploitation of our project and its outcomes. 2.2 MEASURES TO MAXIMIZE IMPACT  
We have already planned a credible path to maximize the impact of our project actions. This plan is comprised of five primary and interdependent elements, namely: (i) specification of target groups, (ii) dissemination activities, (iii) communication channels and activities, (iv) exploitation strategy for market penetration, (v) intellectual property and regulatory assessment.  
Before describing in detail the individual elements, it is noted that both dissemination and communication activities will be led by ISWA, the largest waste management network worldwide, with the mission to promote and develop sustainable and professional waste management and catalyse the transition to a circular economy. ISWA has already established an extensive network of different stakeholders in the waste management sector that will be actively informed about RECLAIM, its activities and results. Along this line, we will regularly present our project in the annual ISWA World Congress which is the industry’s leading solid waste management event. Exploiting direct access to the focused, global waste management and circular economy community, we will assure maximum impact for the proposed work and high potential to promptly include prMRF in the material recovery technologies used globally.  
It is noted that, during the last phase of our project (Wrap up & Consolidation phase), we will consider the transfer and live demonstration of the prMRF in the ISWA World Congress and/or other high-visibility exhibitions in the circular economy domain. This unique demonstration opportunity will be possible due to the portability and quick installation of the prMRF that permits the implementation of demonstration tours to increase project visibility and spark the interest of stakeholders and investors to prMRF and the relevant project technologies.  
Exploitation activities will be led by AXIA, which has long experience in designing strategies and implementing activities that overcome the barriers for the market uptake of research results. Before the end of our project, AXIA will organize a major event to present RECLAIM results to stakeholders and demonstrate prMRF to investors and potential customers.

2.2 Measures to maximise impact - Dissemination, exploitation and communication Our RECREATE strategy for Communication, Dissemination & Exploitation (CDE) aims at supporting the achievement of the Expected Outcomes, within our project lifetime, and facilitating the achievement of the medium-long term Wider Impacts, beyond our project. Our communication activities will generate awareness of our project among different stakeholders and interest in obtaining the disseminated results. The management of research data and IPR will guide our dissemination activities to serve the academic community while protecting our consortium’s interest in commercial exploitation. Dissemination and exploitation of our project results will be based on large-scale demonstration action of cross-sectorial circular value-chains for the recovery and re-use of FRC into high added value products. Among the main existing barriers, our D&E actions will address (i) the lack of circular business models for boosting profitability; (ii) the poor customer acceptance for remanufactured products. Main goals of our CDE workpackages (WP7 -8) will be (i) facilitating the industrial uptake within local eco-systems; (ii) de-risking future industrial uptake through private investments; (iii) increasing awareness for a more conscious consumer behaviour. Large-scale replication and multiplication will take advantage of the Vanguard Initiative “De-and Remanufacturing for Circular Economy” pilot network. 

2.2.1 Exploitation of our project results Our CDE plan and the related process will be led by G_MEDIA, our RECREATE CDE Manager (Section 3). The company has consolidated expertise in engaging, training and support capacity building with relevant stakeholders brought to the dedicated unit Circular Business Academy (www.circularbusiness.academy) and to the development of the Roadmap Towards the Circular Economy in Slovenia. In the implementation of the exploitation part of our plan (WP8), our RECREATE exploitation team will coordinate us and define the organisational aspects needed to address the market. The process will be based on 4 workshops, namely “WS1: Industrial thematic workshops with potential stakeholders”, “WS2: KER exploitation workshop and dedicated exploitation session”, “WS3: Exploitation implementation workshops”, “WS4: How to pitch” workshop. These workshops will provide each demo-case with the opportunity to work on (i) the identification/grouping of key exploitable results; (ii) the first definition of the related use mode; (iii) the identification and mapping of risks related to the exploitation; (iv) follow-up actions. These WS will follow the path tracked by the Lean Canvas approach that will be used for the Business and Use Model Development of the KERs. A crucial step for exploitation is the identification of the risk factors hampering future use, the evaluation and prioritization of the risk factors and the identification of actions to minimize their impact. Risk factors will be screened following these categories: Partnership, Technological, Market, IPR, Environmental and Financial risks. We aim at fostering competitiveness and growth and increasing benefits to us and to the EU economy and citizens. Depending on the project result type, we have developed an innovative method for identifying KERs in a comprehensive and systemic way, targeting different exploitation routes customized to the specific demonstration strategies. Moreover, we have analyzed all the exploitable solutions generated at value-chain level and identified four stakeholder groups for exploitation: 

• Technology and B2B-service providers – TP - and Design & Engineering groups – DE -, which are interested in selling the solutions (or the use of the solutions) and new designs developed in RECREATE to the largest possible target end-user groups, contributing to a sustainable transition to Circular Economy. 
• Recyclers and Remanufacturers – RR - (Lead-users), recycling companies and remanufacturing/repair companies interested in using the new demand-driven recovery and remanufacturing solutions developed by RECREATE for (i) selling recovered components/materials to end-users (ii) selling services to end-users. 
• Product Manufacturers and B2C -service providers – MFG - (End-users): The manufacturers, which are interested in making a concrete transition towards circular economy business models which can enhance the ability to decrease the costs of composite materials, to propose new customized “green” products to the market, and to contribute to the environmental sustainability of their production processes.

2.2.1.1 Preliminary Business Plans for our RECREATE Exploitable Products and Enabling Technologies Individual Partner Business Plan – HEAD Sport GmbH: Our main industrial interest is to validate the re-use of CFs in padel and pickle-ball rackets (Table 9). The flat surfaces require isotropic properties and the use of recycled material from the early beginning of these growing markets will set the standards. Yet the technical requirements of these impact sports remain high and the material needs to comply with them. The result must be highly competitive against current solutions under development. It is assumed the lower costs of the material allow additional production steps necessary for working with recycled fibers. For recycled GFs, our interest is to replace a fleece with rGFs, which is in nearly every ski produced. The fleece must provide interlaminar strength. To achieve this, there is a potential rGF use of 20-25% of total GF volume used. The introduction of recycled materials should not exceed current target cost.
